guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Oral & Dental / Dermatology: primary herpetic gingivostomatitis (oral herpes simplex virus infection) — oral antiviral for severe primary infection and supportive care
Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Fluid & electrolyte therapy: assessment and management of dehydration / inability to maintain oral intake
ASCIA / dermatology guidance on erythema multiforme major and Stevens-Johnson syndrome as the important mucocutaneous mimic to exclude
intentional_test_features: High-risk severe presentation of a usually-trivial-sounding condition: the folder label is 'cold sores', but this is a SEVERE PRIMARY oral herpes simplex infection (primary herpetic gingivostomatitis) causing complete odynophagia, ~18 hours of zero oral fluid intake and objective early dehydration (tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, reduced urine, weakness). The test is that the system must NOT under-triage this to routine 'cold sore self-care' — the correct answer recognises the functional emergency (cannot maintain hydration) and escalates to urgent same-day in-person assessment, not home self-care.

Telehealth management-limit is the driver of the tier (not the diagnosis): the in-person clinician's disposition worked precisely BECAUSE they could deliver topical anaesthesia and personally supervise a fluid trial, then prescribe. Breath-Ezy cannot administer viscous lidocaine, cannot supervise a fluid trial, cannot prescribe autonomously and cannot give IV fluids — so a patient who currently cannot swallow ANY fluids and is dehydrating needs urgent same-day in-person care (T3), escalating to ED for IV rehydration (T4) if unable to tolerate fluids or deteriorating.

Two must-not-miss mimics that telehealth cannot examine for: (1) a severe mucocutaneous drug reaction — Stevens-Johnson syndrome / erythema multiforme major (screen for a new culprit drug, skin target lesions, and eye/genital mucosal involvement); (2) a deep-space / airway process (retropharyngeal or peritonsillar infection, epiglottitis) presenting with odynophagia and drooling (screen for breathing difficulty, stridor, muffled voice, trismus, neck swelling). Both drive prominent mandatory escalation edges.

Over-triage guardrail in the other direction: the patient is haemodynamically stable (no airway compromise, alert, SpO2 normal), so the proportionate telehealth answer is urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3) with an ED/T4 escalation if hydration cannot be maintained — NOT a reflexive 000 ambulance (T5) for a stable patient without airway or circulatory collapse.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who administered viscous lidocaine, personally supervised a 200 mL fluid trial, measured vitals, examined the oral cavity in detail and palpated regional lymph nodes. The AI Doctor cannot do any of these — it is scored on recognising a severe primary oral HSV infection with dehydration from history/video, screening the SJS and airway/deep-space mimics, and arranging urgent in-person care for the supervised fluid trial, prescription antiviral and topical analgesia, with ED escalation for failure to rehydrate.
Age inferred as a young adult (~24) from the source's 'young adult' descriptor and 68 kg body weight with independent baseline function — flagged; adult, so no paediatric dosing issue.

Sex not stated in the source note and inferred with NO firm basis — recorded only because the schema requires sex_at_birth; flag for clinician confirmation. Does not affect the diagnosis, tier or scoring.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-023.
